fr:mainmatter.html:p:
I will be reading and reviewing ~2ish paper a week (tentatively). More accurately, I have allocated roughly 8hrs a week to reading papers. However much I accomplish in this time will determine the actual rate of these reviews. These reviews are mainly just for my own understanding. Rather than techinical details, I will aim to cover
Existing / related work and their issues.
Key components of the methodology and how it solves these issues.
Limitations / future directions or relationships to my work.
For more in-depth coverage of papers I find particularly interesting, see my blog. There will likely be overlap i.e. most blogs will start here...
See upcoming papers!
Notes on the paper Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need.

For,
b batch dimension,
n sequence length,
d hidden dimension,
h number of heads. The total FLOPS for attention are roughly bnd^2. This comes from
3nd^2 FLOPS to compute Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V.
Need to do this b times for each input in batch. and the total memory accesses are roughly \underbrace {bnd}_{X} + \underbrace {bhn^2}_{\text {softmax}} + \underbrace {d^2}_{\text {projection}}. This gives high arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {h}{d} + \frac {1}{bn}\right )^{-1}\right )

However, if we do incremental inference then we must multiply our total number of memory accesses by n i.e. bn^2d + nd^2. This gives arithmetic intensity O\left (\left (\frac {n}{d} + \frac {1}{b}\right )^{-1}\right ), which requires large batch and short sequence length.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: I believe we just ignore the softmax memory contribution in the inference case because it does not scale with n anymore as we are computing the logits just for the next token. Therefore, it becomes a \frac {d}{h} term, which we can safely ignore?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The key is that the \frac {n}{d} term comes from the bn^2d term, where we are loading h (b \times n \times d / h) K and V matrices n times. Thus, we can improve the \frac {n}{d} term by a factor of h by simply not using a different K and V matrix for each head. This is the entire idea behind MQA.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Similar to the KV-cache, we still can use h Q matrices because we do not need to load n of them into memory because only the last one matters for inference.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Group query attention is the same idea as MQA but instead of using one K and V for every head, they use a group of them. Where obviously the number of groups needs to be less than the number of heads.

html:figure.html:figcaption: Grouped query uses subset of K and V matrices.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Also inspired by this idea of reducing the dependence of K and V on the sequence length is sliding window attention.

The idea behind sliding window attention is to ensure the number of weight matrices needed for the keys K and values V does not scale with the sequence length. Intuitively, this means allowing each word to "attend" to only some fixed number of previous words rather than the whole sequence

html:figure.html:figcaption: "the" only sees "on" and "sat" rather than the full sentence.

fr:title.text: Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Fast Transformer Decoding: One Write-Head is All You Need
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @misc{shazeerFastTransformerDecoding2019, title = {Fast {{Transformer Decoding}}: {{One Write-Head}} Is {{All You Need}}}, author = {Shazeer, Noam}, year = {2019}, doi = {10.48550/arXiv.1911.02150}, urldate = {2025-07-14}, number = {arXiv:1911.02150}, publisher = {arXiv}, file = {/home/kellen/Downloads/pdfs/storage/BQ6F9K2H/Shazeer - 2019 - Fast Transformer Decoding One Write-Head is All Y.pdf}, keywords = {Computer Science - Computation and Language,Computer Science - Machine Learning,Computer Science - Neural and Evolutionary Computing}, langid = {english}, archiveprefix = {arXiv}, abstract = {Multi-head attention layers, as used in the Transformer neural sequence model, are a powerful alternative to RNNs for moving information across and between sequences. While training these layers is generally fast and simple, due to parallelizability across the length of the sequence, incremental inference (where such paralleization is impossible) is often slow, due to the memory-bandwidth cost of repeatedly loading the large "keys" and "values" tensors. We propose a variant called multi-query attention, where the keys and values are shared across all of the different attention "heads", greatly reducing the size of these tensors and hence the memory bandwidth requirements of incremental decoding. We verify experimentally that the resulting models can indeed be much faster to decode, and incur only minor quality degradation from the baseline.}, primaryclass = {cs}, eprint = {1911.02150}, month = {November}, shorttitle = {Fast {{Transformer Decoding}}} }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This section is in large part to gaslight myself on the internet into being more productive but with the information provided let us introspect on what happened this week.

Coding: I will now proceed to justify and provide excuses on what went wrong this week. On Monday, we see that I spent a large part of my time on this forest. However, this was actually writing up my notes on lecture 3 of CS336. My goal of roughly 1ish hour of miscellaneous learning a day snowballed into what was seemingly 4 hours of random activity, where I spiraled into MQA. I am thinking of putting this self-study on the backburner in favor of focusing on three easy pieces until the semester starts. I intend to take two courses on what is essentially LLMs systems this fall (very excited), so I might as well not double dip now. I am thinking about using projects 1 and 2 of this course to develop my own working LLM implementation. I can then use it as reference to try out and learn any new optimization tricks like MQA or KV-cache, etc.
These plots also finally confirm what I noticed as a general trend in my working life. Namely, I spend a lot of time working Monday-Thu because of my weekly meetings with my advisor on Thursday. However, I do not properly make use of the rest of the week. This is a trend I hope to improve upon in future entries.

Rest of (computer) life: This week I did a pretty good job staying on task (at least on my computer). Very little brainrot was consumed and a decent amount of work was accomplished. Tune in next week to see if we can keep that up!

Unfortunately, I missed one run this week. The same one I miss every week: Sunday. I will still blame the shin splints, but this may be the last week I can do so (finally!). I am still maintaining a pretty good SPM, but I need to get some more lower HR runs in. From here on out, we should not dip below 20 miles a week. I have some travel coming up at RLC, but I should be able to still run while I am there.
This is another section, where I gaslight myself into some accountability on the internet.

For my imaginary audience, you should have a lot to look forward to in the next week. Namely,
Look forward to an in-depth blog on flow matching to accompany my slides.
I also should have two new research bible entries one on the forward backward representation and the other on hierarchical rl.
For my OS adventure, you should see me (hopefully) get through memory virtualization. However, I should definitely get to at least the TLB. Unlikely, I will continue to do all the exercises (because I spend more time writing them down than doing them). I might do one or two of the projects, but they are quite intimidating TBD. Check back in next week to see if I am productive! Also TBD if in the future I will put research-related stuff in this review.

This is my forest (shoutout to Jon Sterling who is remarkably helpful). This website functions both as a personal knowledge management system, where I store my notes in notebooks and also have some additional external resources in /dev/null. Additionally, I store my thoughts in a variety of consumption mediums. Namely, short-form notes / blog posts can be found in the "marginalia". I also maintain two weekly posting obligations: a weeknotes / lifelog and a paper of the week.
Here I am storing interesting papers I come across and want to review at some point.

fr:title.text: Near-Optimal Representation Learning for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Near-Optimal Representation Learning for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @misc{nachumNearOptimalRepresentationLearning2019, title = {Near-{{Optimal Representation Learning}} for {{Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning}}}, author = {Nachum, Ofir and Gu, Shixiang and Lee, Honglak and Levine, Sergey}, year = {2019}, urldate = {2024-11-07}, number = {arXiv:1810.01257}, publisher = {arXiv}, file = {/home/kellen/Downloads/pdfs/storage/SIK848RC/Nachum et al. - 2019 - Near-Optimal Representation Learning for Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning.pdf}, keywords = {Computer Science - Artificial Intelligence}, langid = {english}, archiveprefix = {arXiv}, abstract = {We study the problem of representation learning in goal-conditioned hierarchical reinforcement learning. In such hierarchical structures, a higher-level controller solves tasks by iteratively communicating goals which a lower-level policy is trained to reach. Accordingly, the choice of representation -- the mapping of observation space to goal space -- is crucial. To study this problem, we develop a notion of sub-optimality of a representation, defined in terms of expected reward of the optimal hierarchical policy using this representation. We derive expressions which bound the sub-optimality and show how these expressions can be translated to representation learning objectives which may be optimized in practice. Results on a number of difficult continuous-control tasks show that our approach to representation learning yields qualitatively better representations as well as quantitatively better hierarchical policies, compared to existing methods (see videos at https://sites.google.com/view/representation-hrl).}, primaryclass = {cs}, eprint = {1810.01257}, month = {January} }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Taking requests below!

This is my blog, in which I write about a variety of topics including computer science, mathematics, and more. For more short-form blogs, see the marginalia. See upcoming blogs and feel free to request! This blog also has a corresponding Atom feed.
This will also serve as a bit of notes from A Primer on Memory Consistency and Cache Coherence
Syntactical things I need to remember from Murphi docs. Most things are straightforward, but there are a few things to note:
Declarations. A declaration is a variable or a collection of variables that are used in the rules.
Rules. A rule is a transition from one state to another

html:ul.html:li: Each rule can optionally have an initial condition required to be fired
We can easily define rules for all the processors or collection of n things with ruleset.
For our use case, rules are used to define

html:ol.html:li: Receiving a request from the network

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Startstates. A startstate is the intial configuration for all our declarations.

html:ul.html:li: Must assign a value to every global variable

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Invariants. An invariant is something that Murphi checks to ensure is true at all possible configurations of the system.
Overview straight from the docs. Repeat forever:

html:ol.html:li: Find all rules whose conditions are true in the current state. (i.e. conditional expressions are true, given the current values of the global variables).
Choose one arbitrarily and execute the action, yielding a new state.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
The first thing we need to do is understand all the things that can go awry.
First in the valid-invalid protocol example we were given they only have two virtual channels. However, we need 3 channels to handle, request messages, forwarded messages, and response messages.
Let us briefly understand why this is the case. Detecting a deadlock is equivalent to detecting a cycle in a dependency graph, so it is sufficient to show a cyclic dependency.
If we issue a GetS this goes to the directory, giving us an edge N1 -> D.
Then the directory issues a Fwd-GetS to the ownder, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2
The owner then needs to send data to the requester, giving us the chain N1 -> D -> N2 -> N1.
Namely, the data must go through the same channel as all of the requests depending on it.
To avoid this, we partition into 3 channels:
Request: GetS, GetM, PutM
Forwarded: Fwd-GetS, Fwd-GetM, PutAck, Inv
Response: InvAck, Data
We directly enumerate these in our MessageType as MessageType: enum { GetS, -- request for shared copy GetM, -- request for modified (exclusive) copy PutS, -- evict shared copy PutM, -- evict modified copy (w/ data)
FwdGetS, -- forwarded GetS to owner FwdGetM, -- forwarded GetM to owner Inv, -- invalidation request
Data, -- data response InvAck, -- invalidation acknowledgment PutAck -- put acknowledgment }; Cache controller. We will start with MSI cache controller, given by the following table:
Notably, we are missing some of the messages in the figures below. We can handle these based on the AckCount ProcState: Record state: enum { P_Invalid, P_Shared, P_Modified, --stable states PT_IS_D, --sent GetS, waiting for Data PT_IS_DI, --sent GetS, got Inv, waiting for Data then go to I PT_IM_AD, --sent GetM (from I), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_SM_AD, --sent GetM (from S), waiting for Data and InvAcks PT_IM_A, --got Data for GetM, waiting for remaining InvAcks PT_MI_A, --sent PutM, waiting for PutAck PT_SI_A, --sent PutS, waiting for PutAck PT_II_A --waiting for PutAck, already invalidated }; val: Value; ack: AckCount; -- tracks InvAck count End;
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark The load, store, and replacement all only alter the states of the coherence protocol for the permanent states. Therefore, to simulate the system it is sufficient to only implement the rules that initiate these state transitions i.e. evict in modified state or load from invalid state.
Directory controller. Now for the directory controller:
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark We actually end up adding a second transient state to the directory controller M^D. This is to handle the PutM, GetM race described below.
Weird Race Conditions
Kellen Kanarios2026324Problem When implementing the PutM rule to downgrade from modified to invalid. We must NOT get rid of the data until we have been acknowledged, so that we can still service forward requests. This is a transition to MI^A. However, we can still receive the PutAck causing us to transition to I before servicing the Fwd-GetM request. This is lost data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Solution We must add a M^D state to the directory controller. In this state, if we get a PutM request from a node that is not our current owner we stall and wait for data to arrive. For M^D to transition to M, we must send the data back to the directory even though we are doing a three-hop, so that the directory knows the data has also been forwarded to the new owner. It is unclear to me the best way to do this without this state.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Problem
The above solution causes a deadlock, where two processors both store miss and one load misses. The "winning processor" of the store will be the second one. The first one will get the data from the directory transitioning from IM^{AD} \to IM^{A}, but the second one will then trigger a Fwd-GetM overwriting the first.
The load miss transitions to IS^D. However, when it receives an Inv it stalls according to the above figure. This blocks the Fwd-GetM from reaching the other winning processor because they are on the same VC. Thus, we have a deadlock where the owner never gets the data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324SolutionWe need to add another transient state not included in the figure. Namely, we add II^{A} that says we were in IS^{A}, but we got invalidated and now we are waiting for our PutAck before we transition to I.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark When the home directory is in M and we get a GetS we cannot clear owner as directed by figure. Otherwise in S^D, we will not be able to tell if the PutM is from the owner and stall, so we only remove the owner once we have received the data.

Aside from the modifications discussed above, the rest was straightforward and we now are ready to move on to MESI.
Cache controller. We will start with MESI cache controller, given by the following table:
Design choice 1. Since we allow immediate upgrade to M, without forwarding, if E upgrades to M, then the value in the directory sent to the next GetS / GetM would not have updated value. It seems we must either
not allow E to silently upgrade to M and then we do not need forwarding, or
allow E to silently upgrade to M and treat E as M basically and forward everything. Notably, neither of these have to write back on PutE, but we will go with 1 because that is how it is indicated in the figure.
Design Choice 2. In order to indicate that the directory received the first GetS we introduce a ExclusiveData message. Upon receiving, ExclusiveData in IS^D we transition to E.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Remark Once we have sent the PutE, or the PutM EI^A and MI^A are functionally the same. I include both for completeness but verified that transitioning directly to MI^A from E works the same.
Directory controller. We start with the analagous table.
Similar to the cache controller, we also add a E^D state for completion that can be absorbed into M^D introduced in the MSI protocol.
Weird Races.
Kellen Kanarios2026324ProblemFirst processor does GetS and is in IS^D but the Home directory sends it exclusive data and is now in E. Then second processor does GetM and Home issues Fwd-GetM to processor one, which arrives before data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324SolutionNeed to stall on Fwd-GetM and Fwd-GetS in IS^D until data arrives
Kellen Kanarios2026324ProblemAn even crazier race happens when we get invalidated before the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS arrive. Due to our previous design decision, we transition to II^A, where if we receive a PutAck we transition to invalid. This then loses the needed forward data.
Kellen Kanarios2026324Example The timeline:
Proc_1 performs a load miss → IS^D
Home receives first GetS from Proc_1 → E and sends ExclusiveData to Proc_1
Proc_2 issues GetM → IM^AD
Home sends Inv to Proc_1
Home forwards Fwd-GetM to Proc_1
Proc_1 receives Inv → II^D
Proc_1 receives ExclusiveData → I Data is lost
Kellen Kanarios2026324Solution We once again have to use the ExclusiveData message. We can then see that we got ExclusiveData in II^D and transition to E instead of I. E will then correctly handle the Fwd-GetM or Fwd-GetS.
We are left with the following state diagrams for the cache and directory controllers respectively

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here are the notes of a selected few of the talks in the course Action and Perception taught by Stella Yu.

fr:title.text: Towards Physically Grounded Digital Twins and Beyond

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Towards Physically Grounded Digital Twins and Beyond

fr:mainmatter.html:p: 3D-reconstruction is not actionable i.e. we can reconstruct a room with a cup but if you try to lift the cup the whole image moves (cup is attached to counter).

html:ol.html:li: Anchor gaussians around the zero level-set
Scene decomposition. Identify interactable and actionable objects
Use LLM for reasoning over objects i.e. density, types of joints
Recover invisible regions via differentiable rendering + generative modeling

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Real-to-sim-to-real: record environment -> build simulation of environment -> train in simulator -> deploy in real world.
Ideas:

html:ul.html:li: Find corresponding actions that match object states in trajectory.

html:ol.html:li: Still not robust for precise movements.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: RL -> Imitation Learning

html:ol.html:li: Do imitation learning on noisy trajectories.
Use noisy robot and human trajectories
Use classifier to try to predict whether trajectory is human or robot

html:ul.html:li: Noise here is the end noise in the diffusion training phase.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Upshot is that they can "augment" robot data with human data in the diffusion training phase by including human trajectories when sampling high noise.
Locomotion tasks: can humanoids move around? Vary terrain, avoid obstacles.
Loco-manipulation tasks: can humanoids interact with the environment?
Other manipulation / locomotion benchmarks are too trivial to see drastic performance between algorithms.
Learning algorithms struggle with large action space
Action space - number of degrees of freedom. Humanoid w/o hands - 19dof, w/ hands 61dof.
Idea: decouple dof. Problem want whole body control.
Idea: hierarchical learning via domain knowledge. learn to position hands. Extended to library of skills in Skillblender.
Robot / environment:
Reward

html:ul.html:li: stay away from things you cannot identify.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Simulator: training on cpu takes forever (enter IsaacGym, Mujoco Playground).
Mujoco Playground limitations

html:ul.html:li: Computation time scales with the number of potential contacts
MJX warp is now supported (100x speedup)

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
Fast TD3
FastTD3 vs. TD3
Massively parallel environments
Large-batch training
Distributional RL
Discretize value space and output distribution over values
Trained with cross entropy loss Body Tranformer,
Inductive bias
Computationally efficient
Multi-frequency reasoning
Localized credit assignment
Idea: construct graph to connect certain parts of body via embodiment graph. Have attention layers that only attend to neighbors on the graph via the mask being a graph mask.
Mask is sparse, allowing much more computationally efficient attention computation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Future directions:
The pipeline
We have the following taxonomy for robot learning:

html:ol.html:li: Autonomous driving: very perception heavy problem -> Learning for perception
Locomotion: very controls-heavy problem -> Learning for control
Manipulation: perception + controls -> End-to-end visuomotor policy learning

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Specialist models are brittle i.e. sensitive to differences in lighting or camera angle.
More data -> generalist models
Predict "action chunks" i.e. a sequence of actions (roughly 1sec into the future)
Use imitation learning: \max _{\theta } \sum _{(a,o,T)} \pi _{\theta }(a \mid o, T) where T is the task identifier.
Datasets: DROID, Open-X embodiment.
Vision-Language-Action model: given images, text description output actions dx, dy, dz.
How do we output actions? Discretization via uniform bins.

html:ul.html:li: Two stream encoder for spatial (DinoV2) and semantic (CLIP) features.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: RMA: in-context locomotion policies.
Models get worse with history. Its own actions are out of distribution, causing it to quickly see out of distribution data.
Simulation currently is only good for simulating rigid objects.

VLAs are still capable of outputting language. Can use this to produce chain of thought output to explain the action. People have found this increases generalization capabilities (see Embodied Chain of Thought Learning).
Two main considerations
OpenVLA models degrade as you increase the control frequency.

html:ul.html:li: Training in naive way, resort to "token-copying".

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Idea: compress actions. Discrete cosine transform.
FAST:
Autoregressive decoding is slow AF:

html:ul.html:li: Diffusion to get the whole chunk at once.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Train and inference via doing BOTH at the same time.

html:ul.html:li: Freeze action expert gradients to VLM.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: How to get started with (scalable) robotics research.

All sorts of resources:
Droid, OpenX
OpenPI
RoboArena

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Presentation given by Tamar Rott Shaham.

fr:title.text: Interpreting the role of a single neuron

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Interpreting the role of a single neuron

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is done in Network dissection
Idea 1: find neuron with maximum activation for subsets of image. Need subset dataset.
Problem: How do you do this for all of the neurons in your network? This is not scalable!
Approach 1: use segmentation network to segment images and use these as subsets. This requires the concepts to be detectabl by a segmentation model.
This is done in MILAN
Approach 2: train a "captioner" model i.e. an LSTM to generate captions for parts of images. Can also get labels using clips as done in CLIP-dissect
Problem with these approaches is they are one-shot i.e. we train the model to produce labels and use those in one-shot. We instead want a "loop" that allows us to refine labeler. Namely

html:ol.html:li: Train model to label something like "human faces"
Find sensitive neuron to images labeled "human faces" by the labeler.
Remove the neuron and see if the model becomes unable to detect human faces.
If this isn't the case then we need to refine the labeler.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: How can we automate this process?

fr:title.text: Automating scientific explorations of neural networks

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Automating scientific explorations of neural networks

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We want to be able to
Observation: LLMs can draw via code see Sparks of AGI.
ViperGPT: Given an image reasoning task, an LLM can write code to solve the image reasoning task
Given a picture of kids with some amount of muffins, answer the question
How many muffins can each kid have for it to be fair?
The LLM writes code to identify children / muffins in the image, counting them, and dividing number of muffins by number of kids.
MAIA API:

html:ul.html:li: takes image, language description along with model and specific neuron i.e. "neuron 0 layer 4" and it will get the activation for the subset of the image focusing on the language description.
Can also take a dataset, or generate new images to test hypothesis.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: End to end user can ask "what function does neuron 122 perform" and MAIA will be able to answer.
How do we know the description is correct? One approach is "synthetic neurons". Provide the value of the neuron as the confidence score from SAM for a given language concept description.

Another approach: take the description given by MAIA, then generate images from the prompts along with "negatives" that have nothing to do with the label of MAIA. Then look at the activation value for each of the examples.

fr:title.text: Auditing and editing neural networks based on interpretations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Auditing and editing neural networks based on interpretations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For classifiers, can check the bias in the logit neurons. For one example, found beer detection class had a bias toward the gender of the person in the image i.e. low probability for females with beer glass.
Can identify and remove biased neurons. Re-train only selective neurons on biased dataset and can match performance of classifier trained on unbiased dataset.
Presentation on PEVA. Related work COSMOS.

Observation: you can only completely predict changes caused by your own actions. You cannot necessarily predict changes caused by the world. \text {Prediction} = \text {change via your actions} + \text {change via the world}
Problem formulation: given history frames, actions predict what is going to happen in pixel space.
Challenges:

html:ol.html:li: There is a strong contextual dependence for both action and vision. Depending on context, you may hold / see things in a different way.
Human actions are harder than global condition. Language vs. high DoF control.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Auto-Regressive diffusion model. Spatial-attention on image space, causal attention on temporal space.
Need architecture, so that actions only interact with next image.
Adaptive layer-norm. (1 + \Delta \gamma (c)) \cdot \mathrm {LN}(x) + \Delta \beta (c) Adaptive per-frame layer-norm. (1 + \Delta \gamma (c_t)) \cdot \mathrm {LN}(x_t) + \Delta \beta (c_t)
Using PEVA for control. Other approaches
Visual geometrially grounded transformer (VGGT).
This is a crash course in image/video diffusion models as part of CSE 598
Classic denoising is TOO slow for images
Idea: compress images into latent representation space. Then learn diffusion model on latent space. This is the idea in Stable Diffusion

html:ol.html:li: How do we scale? Use Transformers! Diffusion Transformer (DiT)
Still too slow. Enter flow matching.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: (1) + (2) = Stable Diffusion 3.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Use U-net for image to image architectures.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Much higher dimensionality! 30 frames = 900x FLOPS
Spatial / temporal decomposition. Align your latents.
Stable video diffusion

html:ol.html:li: Start with image model, add temporal layers

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Instead of spatial / temporal decomposition, you can compress in space / time via 3d autoencoder. C-ViVit encoder, Phenaki.
In diffusion, YOU add the noise. In GAN, whats being decoded is not determined by you.

I have been using LazyVim for some time now, but I have now run into issues multiple times where understanding how LazyVim is doing something is far more difficult than if I had written my own setup. I allocated one day for this adventure and really just wanted to make sure I had support for \TeX , python, forester, and C/C++. Due to my (self-imposed) time constraint, I do not have the associated resources linked for each of the things discussed below. At some point, I hope to come back and more thoroughly cover each of the components.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: To my surprise, a lot of the features that I had come to take for granted were actually options set up internally by Lazyvim. For example, I was shocked with 8 space indents!! and I could not even copy from one terminal instance to another... Due to this, I went and found all of the options I liked from Lazyvim and added them to my new configuration in configs/options.lua.
For this, we will be using the defacto standard lazy.nvim. This is actually straightforward and kind of "just works". Just follow the installation guide in their documentation.

This is one of the main motivations for me making the switch. It seems nvim-cmp has finally been replaced with a new blink.cmp, so that is what we will be using.
Kellen Kanarios202534Rebuilding My (Neo)Vim Config From Scratch › Language Server Protocol It turns out there is a lot that goes into getting LSP setup correctly.
First we must actually install the language servers. To do this the easiest way, we use the mason.nvim and mason.nvim-lspconfig plugins. At some point, I might actually figure out how to set up lsp myself without lspconfig but that point is not now.
Through nvim-lspconfig, we can set up each of the servers we want to have LSP support. I just set up clangd, pyright, and texlab. This was a bit ridiculous. The first of many challenges was around import resolution in python. To remedy this, I needed to write a function to find the virtual environment directory and then set the pythonPath to the venv python binary. Previously, I think I was just using pylsp and installing it as a pip package to each python venv. I much prefer the new way, and I think pyright is overall a much better lsp.
Kellen Kanarios202534Rebuilding My (Neo)Vim Config From Scratch › Forester Completion Another necessary completion source for me is the one provided by forester.nvim. Similar to vimtex, the reference completion support is VERY useful. Obviously, I need completion when I am writing this blog!!! This was a little more involved. The first difficulty was that the completion source provided by the forester.nvim plugin was for nvim-cmp. It turns out this is a prevalent enough problem that the author of blink.cmp wrote an additional plugin blink.compat to allow for nvim-cmp completion sources. While this sounds all fine and good, nvim-compat expects plugins that return the completion source themselves, whereas in forester.nvim the completion source is just one submodule of a more feature-rich plugin. To get around this, I needed to look into the blink.compat code and find how they are registering the sources and just do it myself.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Going all the way back to the Gilles Castel blog post, I have always been partial to snippets that auto-expand. I had them set up prior to Lazyvim but with Lazyvim I had resigned to using friendly-snippets with native nvim snippets. Since I was already redoing everything, this time around I decided not to compromise. Once upon a time (right when it came out I think?) I tried out Luasnips, but it seems that they now have far more extensive features. They are also natively supported by blink.cmp!! It feels necessary that I plug the awesome repo that ports the original Ultisnips snippets to Luasnip. With this, I was able to easily add my own forester snippets!!!

A fun little thing that I had been hoping to do for awhile and is finally now possible - I can load latex snippets when inside math environments in forester!!!! To do this, I looked into the forester treesitter grammar and found the corresponding nodetypes for math envs. It was then straightforward to detect whether we were in a math env and to load the associated latex snippets.

There is a very cool add-on to anki called AnkiConnect that has an associated plugin anki.nvim. Basically, AnkiConnects allows you to make requests to Anki and receive/send useful information from/to your decks. Unfortunately, anki.nvim built-in commands didn't seem all that useful to me. However, it provided the necessary infrastructure for me to accomplish my desired workflow.

Namely, I made my own command that queries Anki for the deck names, which you can then pick from using telescope pickers. When you select one it will create a new flashcard in a specified flashcard directory under a directory created based on the deck name. You can then send this card to that deck using the existing AnkiSend command.

I also continued the snippet fun here. When writing Anki cards, you can write latex code between [latex] [/latex] delimiters. I wrote a quick function to detect whether we are in these delimiters and if we are then to load the latex snippets from the previous section. I also added some basic anki filetype plugins to insert things like these delimiters.
